1: Psalm 69 is the second most cited individual psalm in the NT (after Ps 22), with at least eight distinct quotations or allusions. The opening is the urgent lament-cry. הוֹשִׁיעֵנִי אֱלֹהִים כִּי בָאוּ מַיִם עַד-נָפֶשׁ (hôshîʿênî ʾĕlōhîm kî bāʾû mayim ʿad-nāpesh, "Save me, O God, for the waters have come up to my soul/throat") — hôshîʿa (hiphil imperative: save! deliver!): the urgent cry naming the LORD as the only rescuer. Bāʾû mayim ʿad-nāpesh: "the waters have come to the nāpesh" — the nāpesh is both "soul" and "throat" (the life-breath in the throat): water up to the throat is the moment before drowning. The liturgical tune (shôshannîm: "to the tune of Lilies") frames this urgent lament within Israel's worship practice.
2: טָבַעְתִּי בִּיוֹן מְצוּלָה (ṭābaʿtî bîyôn mĕṣûlāh, "I am sinking in the deep mud") — ṭābaʿ: to sink, to be submerged (as in Exodus 15:4: Pharaoh's chariots "sank" in the Red Sea); yāwen: miry clay, swamp-mud (the sucking mud that holds the foot — also Psalms 40:2 where God drew the Psalmist out of it). Mĕṣûlāh: the deep, the abyss. מַיִם עָמְקִים שִׁבֹּלֶת שְׁטָפַתְנִי (mayim ʿāmĕqîm shibbolet shĕṭāpatni, "the deep waters flood over me") — shibbōlet: a flowing stream, a flood-current (the same word as the "Shibboleth" test of Judges 12:6). The image: the Psalmist sinking in the swamp-mud while the flood-current sweeps over him — no ground underfoot and no escape above.
3: יָגַעְתִּי בְקָרְאִי נִחַר גְּרוֹנִי (yāgaʿtî bĕqārʾî niḥar gĕrônî, "I am exhausted from calling out; my throat is raw") — yāgaʿ: to be weary, to be worn out; nāḥar: to be raw, to be parched (the scorched, burned-out throat from continuous crying). כָּלוּ עֵינַי מְיַחֵל לֵאלֹהָי (kālû ʿênay mĕyaḥêl lêʾlōhāy, "my eyes have given out while waiting for my God") — kālāh: to be consumed, to fail (the eyes that have scanned the horizon for God's arrival until they are burned out — Psalms 119:82: "my eyes fail from searching your promise"). Mĕyaḥêl (piel participle: waiting with hope — from yāḥal: to wait in hope): the exhaustion comes not from despair but from sustained, hoping expectation. Voice and eyes are both spent, yet the waiting continues.
4: רַבּוּ מִשַּׂעֲרוֹת רֹאשִׁי שֹׂנְאַי חִנָּם (rabbû miśśaʿărôt rōʾshî śōnĕʾay ḥinnām, "Those who hate me without cause outnumber the hairs of my head") — ḥinnām: without cause, for nothing, gratuitously. John 15:25 quotes this word directly: "They hated me without a cause" — Jesus applies this verse to himself, identifying himself as the righteous sufferer of Ps 69. אֲשֶׁר לֹא-גָזַלְתִּי אָז אָשִׁיב (ʾăsher lōʾ-gāzaltî ʾāz ʾāshîb, "what I did not steal, I must now restore") — a forensic expression: being compelled to pay back (shûb: to return, to restore) what one did not take (gāzal: to rob). The Psalmist bears the punishment for wrongs he did not commit. The NT applies this to Christ's substitutionary suffering: bearing the debt of sins not his own.
5: אֱלֹהִים אַתָּה יָדַעְתָּ לְאִוַּלְתִּי (ʾĕlōhîm ʾattāh yādaʿtā lĕʾiwwaltî, "O God, you know my foolishness") — ʾiwwelet: folly (the moral recklessness — from ʾĕwîl: the fool). The Psalmist makes no claim to innocence before God: the "without cause" hatred of v4 exists even though the Psalmist has genuinely sinned. וְאַשְׁמוֹתַי מִמְּךָ לֹא-נִכְחָדוּ (wĕʾashmôtay mimmĕkā lōʾ-niḵḥādû, "and my sins are not concealed from you") — ʾashmāh: guilt, offense; kāḥad (niphal: to be concealed). The transparency before God — the Psalmist knows that his own guilt is visible to the judge even as he protests that the enemy's hatred is groundless. Both are simultaneously true.
6: אַל-יֵבֹשׁוּ בִי קֹוֶיךָ אֲדֹנָי יְהוִה צְבָאוֹת (ʾal-yēbōshû bî qōwêkā ʾădōnāy yĕhwāh ṣĕbāʾôt, "Let not those who hope in you be disgraced because of me, O Lord GOD of hosts") — the Psalmist's concern is not merely for himself but for those who share his trust in God: his public failure would publicly discredit all who trust the same God. Qāwāh: to hope, to wait with expectation. The full threefold divine name (Lord YHWH of hosts) is the highest possible divine authority. אַל-יִכָּלְמוּ בִי מְבַקְשֶׁיךָ אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (ʾal-yikkālĕmû bî mĕbaqshêkā ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾêl) — kālam: to be put to shame publicly (as opposed to bôsh — kālam has the nuance of being made to look foolish before others). The seekers of God would be publicly humiliated if the psalmist's suffering were read as divine abandonment.
7: כִּי-עָלֶיךָ נָשָׂאתִי חֶרְפָּה (kî-ʿālêkā nāśāʾtî ḥerpāh, "For your sake I have borne disgrace") — nāśāʾ: to carry, to bear (the load-bearing verb — Isaiah 53:4 uses the same verb: the Servant "bore our griefs"). Ḥerpāh: reproach, disgrace, the public shame of the accused. The motivation: the disgrace is borne "for your sake" (ʿālêkā) — the suffering is vicarious in character; it comes because of the Psalmist's identification with God. כִּסְּתָה כְלִמָּה פָּנָי (kissĕtāh kĕlimmāh pānāy, "humiliation has covered my face") — the face — the seat of personal honor in ancient Near Eastern culture — covered/buried under humiliation.
8: מוּזָר הָיִיתִי לְאֶחָי (mûzār hāyîtî lĕʾeḥāy, "I have become a stranger to my brothers") — zār: a stranger, a foreigner (one who is not at home). The estrangement from one's own family as a result of identification with God is the deepest social wound: the covenant community that should be the place of belonging becomes the place of alienation. וְנָכְרִי לִבְנֵי אִמִּי (wĕnokrî libnê ʾimmî, "an outsider to my own mother's children") — nokrî: the foreign one, the one not of the household (a stronger term than zār). John 7:5 notes that Jesus's brothers did not believe in him — the NT applies this estrangement directly to Christ.
9: כִּי-קִנְאַת בֵּיתְךָ אֲכָלָתְנִי (kî-qinʾat bêtĕkā ʾăkālatni, "Zeal for your house has consumed me") — qinʾāh: zeal, jealousy (the passionate, exclusive devotion — as in Numbers 25:11 where Phinehas's "zeal" turned back God's wrath). ʾĀkal: to eat, to consume (the fire-like consuming of zeal — Exodus 24:17: "the LORD appeared... like a consuming fire"). John 2:17 quotes this verse when Jesus cleanses the temple: "His disciples remembered that it was written, 'Zeal for your house will consume me.'" The switch from aorist ("has consumed") to future ("will consume") in John reflects the eschatological intensification — the temple action is the beginning of a consuming that ends at the cross. וְחֶרְפּוֹת חוֹרְפֶיךָ נָפְלוּ עָלָי (wĕḥerpôt ḥôrpêkā nāplû ʿālay, "and the insults directed at you have landed on me") — Romans 15:3 applies this to Christ: "the reproaches of those who reproached you fell upon me."
10: וָאֶבְכֶּה בַצּוֹם נַפְשִׁי (wāʾebkeh baṣṣôm napshî, "When I wept and humbled my soul with fasting") — bākāh: to weep; ṣôm: fasting (the self-humbling discipline — Joel 2:12); ʿinnāh nāpesh: to humble/afflict the soul (the technical language of the Day of Atonement — Leviticus 16:31; Psalms 23:27). וַתְּהִי לַחֲרָפוֹת לִי (wattĕhî laḥărāpôt lî, "that became a source of shame for me") — the reversal: the acts of piety (weeping, fasting) that should have won social sympathy instead became the material for reproach. The righteous disciplines made the Psalmist a spectacle.
11: וָאֶתְּנָה לְבוּשִׁי שָׂק (wāʾetĕnāh lĕbûshî śāq, "I dressed in sackcloth") — śaq: sackcloth, the coarse garment of mourning and penitence (2 Samuel 3:31; Isaiah 37:1). The sackcloth signals the Psalmist's internal state to the community — the visible mourning garment worn as a communal communication of grief. וָאֱהִי לָהֶם לְמָשָׁל (wāʾĕhî lāhem lĕmāshāl, "and I became a laughingstock to them") — māshāl: a proverb, a byword (as in Deuteronomy 28:37; 1 Kings 9:7 — becoming a "byword" means becoming the negative illustration everyone cites). The mourner mocked: the sackcloth meant to elicit sympathy confirms in the observers' minds that the Psalmist is a lost cause.
12: יָשִׂיחוּ בִי יֹשְׁבֵי שָׁעַר (yāśîḥû bî yōshĕbê shāʿar, "Those who sit at the city gate gossip about me") — yāśaḥ: to speak, to meditate aloud; yōshĕbê shāʿar: "those who sit at the gate" — the civic and judicial center of ancient cities (Ruth 4:1-11; Proverbs 31:23). The very place where legal proceedings happen — the seat of official life — has become the place where the Psalmist is discussed as a public scandal. וּנְגִינוֹת שׁוֹתֵי שֵׁכָר (ûnĕgînôt shôtê shêkār, "and I have become the subject of the drunkards' songs") — nĕgînāh: a song, a musical piece. From the city gate's judiciary to the tavern's drunkards, the whole social spectrum has made him the object of derision.
13: וַאֲנִי תְפִלָּתִי-לְךָ יְהוָה (waʾănî tĕpillātî-lĕkā yĕhwāh, "But as for me, my prayer is directed to you, LORD") — the contrastive waʾănî (but I, as for me): despite the universal mockery (vv11-12), the Psalmist's orientation remains fixed on YHWH alone. עֵת רָצוֹן אֱלֹהִים (ʿêt rāṣôn ʾĕlōhîm, "at a time of your favor, O God") — ʿêt rāṣôn: the acceptable/favorable time (Isaiah 49:8: "In the time of favor I have answered you" — Paul cites Isaiah 49:8 in 2 Corinthians 6:2: "Now is the acceptable time"). בְּרָב-חַסְדְּךָ עֲנֵנִי בֶּאֱמֶת יִשְׁעֶךָ (bĕrāb-ḥasdĕkā ʿănênî beʾĕmet yishʿekā, "O God, in your great steadfast love, answer me in the truth of your salvation") — the twin grounds of appeal: the vastness of divine ḥesed (as in Psalms 51:1) and the faithfulness (ʾemet) of divine salvation. The covenant-reliability that makes God's salvation trustworthy, not merely possible.
14: הַצִּילֵנִי מִטִּיט וְאַל-אֶטְבָּעָה (haṣṣîlênî miṭṭîṭ wĕʾal-ʾeṭbāʿāh, "Rescue me from the mire and do not let me sink") — ṭîṭ: mud, mire (the same swamp-mud of v2); ṭābaʿ (now negated in petition): the opening image is petitioned against — what I am sinking in, rescue me from. אִנָּצְלָה מִשֹּׂנְאַי (ʾinnāṣĕlāh miśśōnĕʾay, "let me be saved from those who hate me") — nāṣal (niphal: to be rescued — the passive petition when the Psalmist cannot rescue himself). וּמִמַּעֲמַקֵּי-מָיִם (ûmimmaʿămaqqê-māyim, "and from the deep waters") — maʿămaqqîm: depths (as in Psalms 130:1: "Out of the depths I cry to you, LORD"). The petition covers both the swamp below (mire) and the flood above (deep waters).
15: אַל-תִּשְׁטְפֵנִי שִׁבֹּלֶת מַיִם (ʾal-tishṭĕpênî shibbolet mayim, "Let not the flood sweep me away") — shāṭap: to flood over, to sweep away; shibbōlet: the stream-current (as in v2). וְאַל-תִּבְלָעֵנִי מְצוּלָה (wĕʾal-tiblāʿênî mĕṣûlāh, "let not the depths swallow me") — bālaʿ: to swallow, to engulf (as in Numbers 16:30-32: the earth "swallowed" Korah). וְאַל-תֶּאְטַר-עָלַי בְּאֵר פִּיהָ (wĕʾal-teʾṭar-ʿālay bĕʾêr pîhā, "let not the pit close its mouth on me") — ʾāṭar: to close (the shutting of the pit's mouth above the one thrown in — Genesis 37:24: Joseph thrown into the pit). Three petitions covering the three dangers: flood, depth, and the closure of Sheol.
16: עֲנֵנִי יְהוָה כִּי-טוֹב חַסְדֶּךָ (ʿănênî yĕhwāh kî-ṭôb ḥasdekā, "Hear me, LORD, for your steadfast love is good") — the ground of appeal: the goodness of divine ḥesed. Ṭôb ḥesed: "good steadfast love" — the goodness is both a quality of the love and a statement about the character of a God whose love exceeds rather than just meets obligation (compare Psalms 63:3: "ḥesed is better than life"). כְּרֹב רַחֲמֶיךָ פְּנֵה אֵלָי (kĕrōb raḥămêkā pĕnêh ʾêlay, "in your great compassion, turn toward me") — raḥămîm: the plural of deep compassion (from reḥem: the womb — the maternal tenderness; Isaiah 49:15). The plural intensifies: not a single act of compassion but the full reservoir of divine motherly tenderness turned toward the drowning petitioner.
17: וְאַל-תַּסְתֵּר פָּנֶיךָ מֵעַבְדֶּךָ (wĕʾal-tastêr pānêkā mêʿabdekā, "Do not turn your face away from your servant") — sātar pānîm: "to hide the face" (the technical term for divine withdrawal — Psalms 13:1; Psalms 27:9; Psalms 44:24 — the experience of divine absence). כִּי-צַר-לִי מַהֵר עֲנֵנִי (kî-ṣar-lî mahêr ʿănênî, "I am in great distress — answer me quickly") — ṣar: the narrow/tight place, the anguish of having no room. Mahêr: quickly (the adverb of urgency — not that God's timing is wrong but that the situation is desperate). The "quickly" of the drowning person to the rescuer: not a demand but a description of extremity.
18: קָרְבָה אֶל-נַפְשִׁי גְאָלָהּ (qorbāh ʾel-napshî gĕʾālāh, "Come near to my soul and rescue it") — qārab: to draw near; gāʾal: to redeem, to act as the gōʾêl (the kinsman-redeemer who restores the lost family member — Ruth 4:4-6; Leviticus 25:25). God petitioned to come near and exercise kinsman-redemption on the drowning soul. לְמַעַן אֹיְבַי פְּדֵנִי (lĕmaʿan ʾôyĕbay pĕdênî, "set me free because of my enemies") — pādāh: to ransom, to redeem by payment. The "because of" (lĕmaʿan) is remarkable: the enemies' existence is itself the argument for rescue — God's character is vindicated when the righteous sufferer is rescued from those who wrongly persecute him.
19: אַתָּה יָדַעְתָּ חֶרְפָּתִי וּבָשְׁתִּי וּכְלִמָּתִי (ʾattāh yādaʿtā ḥerpātî ûbāshtî ûkĕlimmātî, "You know my disgrace, my shame, and my humiliation") — three terms for public dishonor accumulating: ḥerpāh (disgrace/reproach — v9), bōshet (shame — the state of being put to shame), kĕlimmāh (humiliation — the being-made-to-look-foolish of v6). God knows all three dimensions of the Psalmist's public destruction. נֶגְדְּךָ כָּל-צוֹרְרָי (negdĕkā kol-ṣôrĕrāy, "all my enemies are in your sight") — neged: before, in plain sight of. The appeal: if God sees it all, let him act on what he sees.
20: חֶרְפָּה שָׁבְרָה לִבִּי (ḥerpāh shābrāh libbî, "Disgrace has broken my heart") — shābar: to break, to shatter (as in Psalms 34:18: "The LORD is near to the brokenhearted" — nishbĕrê lêb). The reproach (ḥerpāh) as the breaking-agent for the heart: the sustained public dishonor has shattered the inner center. וָאֲנוּשָׁה (wāʾănûshāh, "and I am in anguish") — ʾānash: to be mortally ill, to be beyond recovery (Jeremiah 17:9: "the heart is ʾānûsh — desperately sick"; Jeremiah 30:12: a wound that is incurable). וָאֲקַוֶּה לָנוּד וָאַיִן (wāʾăqawweh lānûd wāʾayin, "I looked for someone to show compassion, but there was none") — nûd: to nod in sympathy, to show compassion (Job 2:11: "they went to comfort and console him"). Wayyāyin: and there was none — the absolute absence of any human comforter. The isolation is complete.
21: The most cited verse of Psalm 69 in the Passion narratives. וַיִּתְּנוּ בְּבָרוּתִי רֹאשׁ (wayyittĕnû bĕbārûtî rōʾsh, "They put bitter gall in my food") — bārût: food, bread (the sustaining meal); rōʾsh: gall, head-poison (a bitter, poisonous plant — Amos 6:12: "wormwood"). They put poison in what was meant to sustain. וְלִצְמָאִי יַשְׁקוּנִי חֹמֶץ (wĕliṣmāʾî yashqûnî ḥōmeṣ, "and when I was thirsty, they offered me vinegar to drink") — ḥōmeṣ: vinegar, sour wine (acidic, undrinkable as a refreshment). Matthew 27:34 (gall mixed with wine given to Jesus before crucifixion) and Matthew 27:48/John 19:29 (vinegar on a sponge at the cross) fulfill this verse. John 19:28 explicitly notes that Jesus said "I thirst" "so that the scripture would be fulfilled" — a direct citation of this verse's thirst-and-vinegar scene.
22: יְהִי-שֻׁלְחָנָם לִפְנֵיהֶם לְפָח (yĕhî-shulḥānām lipnêhem lĕpāḥ, "Let their table be set as a snare before them") — shulḥān: a table (the meal, the place of fellowship and covenant); pāḥ: a bird-trap, a snare. The table — the symbol of hospitality and covenant fellowship — becomes a trap. וְלִשְׁלוֹמִים לְמוֹקֵשׁ (wĕlishlômîm lĕmôqêsh, "and what was meant for their prosperity become a trap") — shālôm (plural: prosperity, peace); môqêsh: a snare (Psalms 64:5). Romans 11:9-10 quotes vv22-23 directly, applying them to Israel's hardening: their table (Torah, temple, covenant privilege) has become a trap through rejection of the Messiah. Paul's application is the ultimate reversal: what was given for salvation has been used for stumbling.
23: תֶּחְשַׁכְנָה עֵינֵיהֶם מֵרְאוֹת (teḥshaknahnāh ʿênêhem mêrĕʾôt, "Let their eyes be darkened so they cannot see") — ḥāshak: to be dark, to grow dark (Psalms 18:28: "you lighten my darkness"; Isaiah 5:30: "the light is dark in the clouds"). The darkened eyes of those who refused to see now unable to see at all: the judicial punishment fitting the sin of willful blindness. Romans 11:10 quotes: "Let their eyes be darkened so that they cannot see." וּמָתְנֵיהֶם תָּמִיד הַמְעַד (ûmātnêhem tāmîd hamʿad, "and let their bodies tremble without ceasing") — mātên: the loins/hips (the center of physical strength); maʿad (hiphil: to cause to shake): the shaking of the loins as physical strength fails. Double judgment: vision darkened, physical strength failing — the complete debility of those from whom God withdraws.
24: שְׁפָךְ-עֲלֵיהֶם זַעְמֶךָ (shĕpōk-ʿălêhem zaʿmekā, "Pour out your wrath upon them") — shāpak zaʿam: the pouring out of divine wrath (Jeremiah 10:25: "Pour out your wrath on the nations"; Rev 16: the seven bowls of wrath poured out). Zaʿam: wrath, indignation (the burning anger that scorches what it falls on — distinct from ʾap: the flaring nostril-anger). וַחֲרוֹן אַפְּךָ יַשִּׂיגֵם (waḥărôn ʾappĕkā yaśśîgêm, "let your fierce anger seize them") — ḥărôn ʾap: the burning of the nostrils (Exodus 32:12); nāśag (hiphil: to overtake, to catch up with — as a pursuing force catches the fleeing). The imprecatory petition: the enemies' actions warrant the judgment now called down upon them.
25: תְּהִי-טִירָתָם נְשַׁמָּה (tĕhî-ṭîrātām nĕshammāh, "Let their camp become desolate") — ṭîrāh: encampment, fortified settlement; shāmam (niphal: to be desolate, to be devastated). בְּאָהֳלֵיהֶם אַל-יְהִי יֹשֵׁב (bĕʾōhŏlêhem ʾal-yĕhî yōshêb, "let no one live in their dwelling") — the total emptying of the dwellings. Acts 1:20 quotes this verse directly with reference to Judas Iscariot: "For it is written in the Book of Psalms, 'Let his homestead become desolate, and let there be no one to live in it.'" Peter applies the judicial prayer of the persecuted righteous to the specific betrayer of Jesus, identifying Judas as the embodiment of the enemies of the anointed sufferer.
26: כִּי-אַתָּה אֲשֶׁר-הִכִּיתָ רָדָפוּ (kî-ʾattāh ʾăsher-hikkîtā rādāpû, "For they have harassed the one whom you have afflicted") — nākāh (hiphil: to strike, to smite): God himself has struck the Psalmist (permitted or caused the affliction — Isaiah 53:4: "we esteemed him stricken, smitten by God"). The enemies pile on the one already struck by God — the double wound of divine affliction and human persecution. וְאֶל-מַכְאוֹב חֲלָלֶיךָ יְסַפֵּרוּ (wĕʾel-makʾôb ḥălālêkā yĕsappêrû, "and they gossip about the suffering of those you have wounded") — makʾôb: pain, anguish (from kāʾab: to be pained — Isaiah 53:3-4: the Servant who was "a man of sorrows"). The mockers take the divine wounding as the occasion for gossip rather than compassion.
27: תְּנָה-עָוֹן עַל-עֲוֹנָם (tĕnāh-ʿāwōn ʿal-ʿăwōnām, "Pile their guilt on top of their guilt") — the imprecatory petition: nātan ʿāwōn ʿal ʿāwōn: "give iniquity upon their iniquity" — the judicial principle of compounding guilt for compounded sin. They who persecuted the afflicted one have added offense upon offense; let the accounting reflect the accumulation. וְאַל-יָבֹאוּ בְּצִדְקָתֶךָ (wĕʾal-yābōʾû bĕṣidqātekā, "and do not let them share in your vindication") — ṣĕdāqāh: righteousness, vindication (the divine declaration of right — Isaiah 46:13). Let them have no share in the acquittal that the righteous sufferer receives.
28: יִמָּחוּ מִסֵּפֶר חַיִּים (yimmāḥû missêper ḥayyîm, "Let them be erased from the scroll of the living") — māḥāh (niphal: to be wiped out, to be erased — Exodus 32:32-33 where Moses asks to be "blotted out" of God's book). Sêper ḥayyîm: "the scroll of the living" — the divine register of the living (Exodus 32:32; Isaiah 4:3; Luke 10:20). Revelation 3:5 echoes this verse in the reverse: "I will not blot out his name from the book of life." וְעִם-צַדִּיקִים אַל-יִכָּתֵבוּ (wĕʿim-ṣaddîqîm ʾal-yikkātêbû, "let them not be listed among the righteous") — exclusion from both registers: the scroll of life and the company of the righteous. The two registers are related: to be in the book of life is to be among the righteous; exclusion is double.
29: וַאֲנִי עָנִי וְכֹאֵב (waʾănî ʿānî wĕkōʾêb, "But I am afflicted and in pain") — the contrastive return to the individual sufferer after the imprecatory section: the enemies in their guilt; the Psalmist in his affliction and pain. ʿĀnî: afflicted, lowly; kōʾêb: in pain (from kāʾab: the same word as the Servant's "sorrows" in Isa 53). יְשׁוּעָתְךָ אֱלֹהִים תְּשַׂגְּבֵנִי (yĕshûʿātĕkā ʾĕlōhîm tĕśaggĕbênî, "may your salvation, O God, lift me up") — śāgab (piel: to set on high, to lift to safety — Psalms 59:1; Psalms 91:14). The salvation lifts the lowly to a high secure place — the rescue from the mire of vv2-3 accomplished.
30: אֲהַלְלָה שֵׁם-אֱלֹהִים בְּשִׁיר (ʾăhallĕlāh shêm-ʾĕlōhîm bĕshîr, "I will praise the name of God with a song of praise") — the psalm pivots from lament and imprecation to vow and praise: the personal testimony of the rescued sufferer (as in Psalms 22:22: "I will tell of your name to my brothers"). Hālal (piel: to praise lavishly — the root of hallĕlû-Yāh). וַאֲגַדְּלֶנּוּ בְתוֹדָה (waʾăgaddĕlennû bĕtôdāh, "I will honor him with thanksgiving") — gādal (piel: to make great, to magnify); tôdāh: the thanksgiving offering (Psalms 50:23: "The one who offers the sacrifice of thanksgiving glorifies me"). The magnifying of God is the Psalmist's act of making God's greatness visible and audible to others.
31: וְתִיטַב לַיהוָה מִשּׁוֹר פָּר (wĕtîṭab laYHWH mishôr par, "This will please the LORD more than an ox or a bull") — yāṭab (qal: to be good, to be pleasing): the praise-song will please God more than the largest and most expensive animal sacrifices. מַקְרִן מַפְרִיס (maqrîn maphrîs, "with its horns and hoofs") — qeren: horn (the sign of mature strength and full value in a sacrificial animal); pāras: hoof (split hoof — the clean-animal marker of Leviticus 11:3). The most valuable possible sacrifice: a mature bull with full horns and clean hoofs. The Psalmist claims that heartfelt praise and thanksgiving exceed this. Psalms 50:14 and 51:17 develop the same tradition: "The sacrifice acceptable to God is a broken spirit."
32: רָאוּ עֲנָוִים יִשְׂמָחוּ (rāʾû ʿănāwîm yiśmāḥû, "The afflicted will see it and rejoice") — ʿānāw: the humble, the meek, the afflicted (the same social class as the Psalmist — those who have no earthly recourse). When they see the Psalmist's rescue, they rejoice: the personal testimony of salvation is the communal encouragement of all who suffer similarly. דֹּרְשֵׁי אֱלֹהִים וִיחִי לְבַבְכֶם (dōrshê ʾĕlōhîm wîḥî lĕbabkem, "those of you who seek God, may your hearts be renewed") — dārash: to seek diligently (Psalms 34:4; Amos 5:4: "seek me and live"); ḥāyāh (qal: may your hearts live) — the living of the heart as the opposite of the broken heart (v20). The assembly encouraged by one rescued person's testimony.
33: כִּי-שֹׁמֵעַ אֶל-אֶבְיוֹנִים יְהוָה (kî-shōmêaʿ ʾel-ʾebyônîm yĕhwāh, "For the LORD listens to the needy") — ʾebyôn: the needy, the destitute (from ʾābāh: to desire, to want — the one in want). YHWH hears specifically what no human hears — the cry of the poorest. וְאֶת-אֲסִירָיו לֹא בָזָה (wĕʾet-ʾăsîrāyw lōʾ bāzāh, "he does not look down on those he has bound") — ʾāsar: to bind (his prisoners, his bound ones — those whose affliction comes as if they were prisoners in God's custody); bāzāh: to despise. God does not despise the very ones he has allowed to suffer: their suffering is not evidence of divine contempt but of divine pedagogy. Psalms 22:24 expresses the same truth: "he has not despised or scorned the suffering of the afflicted one."
34: יְהַלְלוּהוּ שָׁמַיִם וָאָרֶץ (yĕhallĕlûhû shāmayim wāʾāreṣ, "Let heaven and earth give him praise") — the cosmic praise (as in Ps 148 — the entire creation summoned). יַמִּים וְכָל-רֹמֵשׂ בָּם (yammîm wĕkol-rōmêś bām, "the seas and every living thing in them") — rōmêś: everything that creeps/moves (Genesis 1:21-26). The scope: heaven, earth, and sea — the three zones of creation all summoned to praise the God who hears the needy. The personal lament psalm broadens at its close to cosmic dimensions: the individual's rescue is an event of universal significance.
35: כִּי אֱלֹהִים יוֹשִׁיעַ צִיּוֹן (kî ʾĕlōhîm yôshîaʿ ṣiyyôn, "For God will save Zion") — the eschatological horizon: the individual's rescue (v29) is a sample of Zion's rescue. Yôshîaʿ (hiphil imperfect: will save — the future certainty based on the divine character). וְיִבְנֶה עָרֵי יְהוּדָה (wĕyibneh ʿārê yĕhûdāh, "and rebuild the cities of Judah") — the rebuilding of destroyed cities (Isaiah 54:3; Psalms 61:4). וְיָשְׁבוּ שָׁם וִירֵשׁוּהָ (wĕyāshĕbû shām wîrêshûhā, "people will live there and take possession of it") — the restoration of the covenant people to their land as the conclusive sign of divine salvation.
36: וְזֶרַע עֲבָדָיו יִנְחָלוּהָ (wĕzeraʿ ʿăbādāyw yinḥālûhā, "The children of his servants will inherit it") — zeraʿ: seed, offspring (the covenant continuity through generations — Genesis 17:7-8: "your seed after you"); nāḥal: to inherit (the covenantal transfer of the land from generation to generation). וְאֹהֲבֵי שְׁמוֹ יִשְׁכְּנוּ-בָהּ (wĕʾōhăbê shĕmô yishkĕnû-bāh, "and those who love his name will live there") — the defining characteristic of the inheritors: not ethnic descent alone but love of the divine name (Psalms 5:11: "let all who love your name rejoice in you"). The psalm that began in drowning waters ends with the seed of servants dwelling securely in an inherited land — the complete reversal of the lament's situation.
